coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots implanted CdS nanosheets: Efficient visible-light-driven photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) under saline conditions

coredata.dc:description: Chromium(VI) (Cr(VI)), a toxic metal, is generally present together with ionic salts in industrial effluents. An efficient reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) in saline water is an imperative issue but still a challenging task. Literature has rarely addressed reducing Cr(VI) effectively under saline conditions. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully implanted in the CdS nanosheets (CdS-NSs) to prepare nanocomposites (i.e., CCNs) for the first time to efficiently reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III). The newly fabricated CCNs demonstrated superior performance to reduce Cr(VI) compared to pristine CdS-NSs and CQDs-deposited CdS nanosheets (i.e., CQD/CdS-NSs) in saline water. The nanostructures were examined by spectral and photoelectrochemical measurements as well as density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Results showed that CCNs facilitated the photo-electron transport and thus suppressed charge recombination via formation of micro-regional heterostructures. A lower band gap of CCNs relative to pristine CdS-NSs and CQD/CdS-NSs extended the light absorption spectrum. The optimal photocatalyst, denoted as CCNs-2, exhibited an efficiency of ∼94% for photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction within 10 min in water containing 1200 mg/L salts. The obtained rate constant of reduction of Cr(VI) was (2.62 ± 0.04) × 10−1 min−1, approximately 4 and 3 times higher than that of pristine CdS-NSs and CQD/CdS-NSs, respectively. After 3 cycles, the CCNs-2 still showed an efficiency of ∼78% aqueous Cr(VI) reduction within 10 min. Our results clearly presented that implantation relative to deposition of CQDs for CdS-NSs is a preferential strategy to enhance photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction in saline water under visible light irradiation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the as-prepared pristine CdS-NSs, CQDs, CCNs, and CQD/CdS-NSs in the present study.
XPS spectra of the as-prepared pristine CdS-NSs, CCNs-2 and CQD/CdS-NSs. (a) Survey. (b) Cd 3d. (c) S 2p. (d) C 1s.

Optimized CQD and three possible CdS coordinated to CQD at B3LYP/3–21 G level. (a) Optimized CQD, Cd-N bond length: 1.374 Å. (b) Structure of CQD-NH2-CdS, the Cd-N bond length: 1.493 Å. (c) Structure of CQD-NH-CdS, the Cd-N bond length: 1.375 Å. (d) Structure of CQD-N-CdS, the Cd-N bond length: 1.320 Å.

HRTEM images of the as-prepared nanostructures in this study. (a) CdS-NSs. (b) CQDs. (c) HRTEM image of CQDs with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm. (d,e,f) CCNs-2, and the (e) is HRTEM image of the red circle in panel (d), and the (f) is HRTEM image of the red square in panel (e). Lattice spacings of 0.22 and 0.33 nm corresponding to CQDs and CdS, respectively. (g,h) CQD/CdS-NSs, and (h) HRTEM image of the red square in panel (g) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Photocatalytic reduction of aqueous Cr(VI) by the as-prepared nanostructures. (a) Photocatalytic reduction of aqueous Cr(VI) by different CQDs-implanted CdS-NSs (i.e., CCNs). (b) Effect of implantation or deposition of CQDs in/on CdS-NSs on the photocatalytic reduction of aqueous Cr(VI). (c) Reaction kinetics for photocatalytic reduction of aqueous Cr(VI) by the as-prepared nanostructures. The photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) followed a pseudo-first-order kinetic model: ln(C0/Ct) = kt. Here C0 is the initial concentration (mg/L) of aqueous Cr(VI), Ct is the concentration (mg/L) of aqueous Cr(VI) at time of t, k is the kinetic rate constant (min−1), and t is the irradiation time (min). (d) Rate constants of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction by the different as-prepared nanostructures. Here CCNs-0.5, CCNs-1, CCNs-2, CCNs-5, and CCNs-10 represented 0.5, 1, 2, 5 and 10 mg of CQDs addition respectively during synthesis of CCNs in the study.

Photocatalytic reduction of aqueous Cr(VI) by the as-prepared CCNs-2 in water with different contents of inorganic salts. (a) 50 mg/L. (b) 500 mg/L. (c) 1200 mg/L. (d) Photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction by pristine CdS-NSs, CCNs-2 and CQD/CdS-NSs in saline water (1200 mg/L CaCl2).
DRS spectra and band gap energies of pristine CdS-NSs, CCNs-2 and CQD/CdS-NSs. (a) DRS spectra. (b) Band gap energies.
EIS Nyquist plots of the as-prepared nanostructures. (a) pristine CdS-NSs and CQD/CdS-NSs. (b) CCNs-2.
XPS valence band spectra (a) and band structure diagram (b) for pristine CdS-NSs, CCNs-2 and CQD/CdS-NSs.
Schematic model for the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) in saline water by CCNs-2 under visible light.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chromium(VI) (Cr(VI)), a toxic metal, is generally present together with ionic salts in industrial effluents. An efficient reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) in saline water is an imperative issue but still a challenging task. Literature has rarely addressed reducing Cr(VI) effectively under saline conditions. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were successfully implanted in the CdS nanosheets (CdS-NSs) to prepare nanocomposites (i.e., CCNs) for the first time to efficiently reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III). The newly fabricated CCNs demonstrated superior performance to reduce Cr(VI) compared to pristine CdS-NSs and CQDs-deposited CdS nanosheets (i.e., CQD/CdS-NSs) in saline water. The nanostructures were examined by spectral and photoelectrochemical measurements as well as density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Results showed that CCNs facilitated the photo-electron transport and thus suppressed charge recombination via formation of micro-regional heterostructures. A lower band gap of CCNs relative to pristine CdS-NSs and CQD/CdS-NSs extended the light absorption spectrum. The optimal photocatalyst, denoted as CCNs-2, exhibited an efficiency of ∼94% for photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction within 10 min in water containing 1200 mg/L salts. The obtained rate constant of reduction of Cr(VI) was (2.62 ± 0.04) × 10−1 min−1, approximately 4 and 3 times higher than that of pristine CdS-NSs and CQD/CdS-NSs, respectively. After 3 cycles, the CCNs-2 still showed an efficiency of ∼78% aqueous Cr(VI) reduction within 10 min. Our results clearly presented that implantation relative to deposition of CQDs for CdS-NSs is a preferential strategy to enhance photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction in saline water under visible light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: Toxic metals in effluents of various industries pose a great threat to ecosystem and public health. Regulations on toxic metals in industrial wastewaters have been increasingly stringent worldwide [1]. Among toxic metals, hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) seriously threatens ecological systems and human health due to its high solubility, acute toxicity, and potential carcinogenicity [2,3]. An allowable maximum contaminant level of Cr(VI) in the discharged industrial effluents has been strictly set in most countries such as U.S.A. and China to ensure water quality of receiving water bodies [4].

A variety of techniques such as adsorption [5], membrane filtration [6], chemical precipitation [7], and electrochemical process have been developed to remove Cr(VI) from wastewater [8]. However, adsorption and membrane filtration may likely re-release Cr(VI) during regeneration of the used membrane and adsorbents [9,10]. Chemical precipitation remains problematic to decrease the levels of Cr(VI) to sub-ppm level due to a poor settleability of precipitate [11]. Electrochemical processes involve high energy consumption. More importantly, Cr(VI) is generally present in saline wastewater (e.g., electroplating and tanning effluents) [12,13]. The co-existence of inorganic salts dramatically compromises Cr(VI) removal efficiency using these techniques [14,15]. Thus, seeking an economical and highly efficient technique to remove Cr(VI) from saline water is pivotal.

In recent years, photocatalytic oxidation/reduction technology has attracted considerable attentions due to avoidance of pollution and energy saving [16–23]. Significantly, an occurrence of photocatalytic evolution of H2 from seawater [24,25] implies potentials of reducing Cr(VI) in saline water via the photocatalytic technique. Inorganic semiconductors, in particular metal sulfides, have been investigated as efficient visible light response photocatalysts [26,27]. Among them, cadmium sulfide (CdS) possesses a narrow band gap (E g = ∼2.4 eV) and suitable band structure, which is capable of effectively utilizing visible light that accounts for ∼43% of the solar spectrum [28,29], making it one of the most popular photocatalyst [30–32]. Given its photocatalytic reduction capability for H2 evolution, CdS could be a potential candidate for photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction. However, an application of pristine CdS to cause water splitting or Cr(VI) reduction is still greatly limited due to rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and severe photocorrosion under visible light irradiation [33–35]. Various strategies have been developed in recent studies to overcome these drawbacks [36–40].

CdS as a semiconductor with special shapes has been prepared [39,40]. Construction of CdS heterostructures has also been synthesized to enhance photoactivity through heterojunctions, which could help to separate photogenerated electrons and holes through interfacial charge transfer [41]. Examples include ZnIn2S4/CdS heterostructures that exhibited an increased visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity and strong durability toward Cr(VI) reduction [42]. However, trace metals like In, Pt and W were consumed during fabrication of heterostructures, which may greatly increase the economic cost [42–44]. This paper presents an alternative carbon-based nanomaterial, i.e., carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which have a great potential to increase photocatalytic activity of CdS.

CQDs have been successfully deposited on functional backbone such as TiO2 or g-C3N4 to improve its photocatalytic activity by decreasing photogenerated electrons and holes recombination and broadening the photo-absorption region [45,46]. Yet, the energy consumption for charges across the barriers between the backbone and deposited CQDs is still too high to be reasonably acceptable [47]. Significantly, implantation may be a better approach than deposition to enhance the photocatalytic activity of CdS and is thus the focus of current study. We hypothesized that CQDs-implanted CdS nanosheets (i.e., CCNs) may provide well interfacial connection, which is very crucial in promoting the charges transfer/separation and thus the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI). Herein, the successful synthesis of CCNs for the first time showed effective photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) in saline water under visible light irradiation.

A strategy of designing and synthesizing CCNs was performed via solvothermal fabrication of freeze-dried CQDs and CdS nanosheet (CdS-NS) precursors. In comparison with pristine CdS-NSs and CQDs-deposited CdS nanosheets (i.e., CQD/CdS-NSs), the CCNs exhibited superior visible-light-driven photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) in saline water. Moreover, the outstanding photocatalytic performance of CCNs was investigated by spectral characterizations, photoelectrochemical measurements and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Results are presented to demonstrate that implantation of CQDs could broaden the photo-absorption region and facilitate the photo-electron transport via formation of micro-regional heterostructures.

Citric acid, sulfur powder, cadmium chloride hemipentahydrate, sodium chloride, sodium nitrate, calcium chloride, sodium dichromate, sulfuric acid, hydrochloride acid, diphenylcarbazide, ethylenediamine, ethanol, diethylenetriamine, acetone, and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Suwannee River Natural organic matter (NOM, 2R101 N) was acquired from the International Humic Substance Society (Denver, U.S.A.). All reagents were used as received without any further purification. The ultrapure water (UPW) used in all experiments was obtained from a Direct-Q system (Millipore, Billerica, MA) with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ/cm.

The CdS-NSs were synthesized using a solvothermal method with minor modifications [48]. Briefly, 370 mg of cadmium chloride hemipentahydrate and 320 mg of sulfur powder were added to 60 mL of diethylenetriamine, followed by ultrasonication at 0 °C (ice-water mixture) for 30 min to achieve a homogeneous dispersion. The mixture was transferred into a 100 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and kept at 80 °C for 3 d. After that, the solid product was collected by centrifugation at 9582 g for 15 min and washed three times with ethanol and one time with UPW. Finally, the obtained precipitate in bright yellow color was dried overnight at 60 °C in a vacuum oven.

The CQDs were synthesized using the method described in previous studies [49,50]. In brief, 2010 mg of citric acid and 670 μL of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 20 mL of UPW. The mixture was transferred to a 50 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 5 h. Then, the transparent solution in brown-black color was subjected to dialysis (3 kDa) in UPW for 48 h to remove impurities. UPW was changed at an interval of 2 h through the dialysis process. The purified solution was freeze-dried under vacuum to obtain the CQDs powder.

The CCNs were prepared through a solvothermal method. Briefly, 370 mg of cadmium chloride hemipentahydrate and 320 mg of sulfur powder were dissolved in 60 mL of diethylenetriamine. An appropriate amount of CQD was then added to the solution, followed by ultrasonication at 0 °C (ice-water mixture) for 15 min. Specifically, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, and 10.0 mg of CQDs were used, and the as-prepared precipitates were denoted as CCNs-0.5, CCNs-1, CCNs-2, CCNs-5, and CCNs-10, respectively. Each mixture was transferred into a 100 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and kept at 80 °C for 3 d. The solid products were collected by centrifugation at 9582 g for 15 min and washed three times with ethanol and one time with UPW. The obtained precipitates were dried overnight at 60 °C under a vacuum oven.

The CQD/CdS-NSs were prepared using a deposited method with minor modifications [51]. Briefly, 180 mg of CdS-NSs was added to 15 mL of ethanol, followed by ultrasonication at 0 °C (ice-water mixture) for 30 min to achieve a homogeneous dispersion. Then, 2.0 mg of CQDs was added to the solution and magnetically stirred for 12 h. Afterward, ethanol was removed by vaporization at room temperature. The obtained precipitate was dried overnight at 60 °C in a vacuum oven.

The morphology of the as-prepared nanostructures was observed by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100 F, Japan) at 200 kV. The lattice fringe spacing was also determined using the HRTEM instrument at 200 kV. The diffuse reflection spectrum (DRS) was measured through a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu). Infrared spectrometer (IR) was performed on a Thermo Nicolet iS10 (Japan) with a diamond attenuated total reflectance (ATR) attachment to qualitative estimation of functional groups on the surface of the as-prepared nanostructures. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of each nanostructure was characterized through a PANalytical X'Pert PRO diffractometer with Cu-Kα-radiation, with the diffraction angle from 10° to 80° at 0.05° per step. To characterize the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs of the as-prepared photocatalysts, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed using a three-electrode system (CHI660E, Chenhua, Shanghai) in 10 mM Na2SO4 solution. The EIS spectroscopy was performed from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz at an open circuit potential of 0.3 V and an alternating current voltage amplitude of 5 mV. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of the as-prepared nanostructures was obtained using a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer (Kratos, Japan) to show the valence states of the constituent elements, and the binding energy was calibrated with the C1s peak at 284.6 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance under visible light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared nanostructures was evaluated through photocatalytic reduction of aqueous Cr(VI) under a simulated solar lamp (300 W xenon lamp, 350–780 nm, CEL-HXF300, China) with a 400 nm cut-off filter. A quartz vessel (100 mL) containing reaction solution was connected to a cooling system to maintain the solution temperature in the light where light intensity was ∼150 mW cm−2. In brief, 12.0 mg of the as-prepared nanostructure was added to 60 mL of 20 mg/L Cr(VI) solution. Prior to visible-light irradiation, the aqueous solution was magnetically stirred for 30 min under dark to establish an adsorption-desorption equilibrium to eliminate the influence of adsorption. During irradiation, a 2.0 mL aliquot of solution was taken at certain intervals (e.g., 0, 1, 5, 10, 15, and 20 min), followed by centrifugation at 14,972 g for 3 min. The concentration of Cr(VI) in the supernatant was measured spectrophotometrically using the standard diphenylcarbazide (DPC) method [52]. Briefly, DPC stock solution was firstly prepared by dissolving 10 mg of DPC solid in 5 mL of acetone containing 50 μL of concentrated sulfuric acid. Then, 4.0 mL of DPC stock solution was added to 100 mL of UPW, achieving a DPC working solution. A 0.5 mL aliquot of supernatant was added to a vial containing 2.5 mL of the DPC working solution. Afterwards, the vial was mixed vigorously and kept standing for 30 min, followed by spectrophotometry analysis at λmax =540 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu).

To investigate the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) in saline water, various inorganic salts (i.e., NaCl, NaNO3 and CaCl2) were gradually added to the reaction solution from 0 to 1200 mg/L. Effects of solution pH (4.1, 6.4, 8.1 and 10.0) and NOM (2, 5 and 10 mg/L) on the photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared nanostructures were also examined. The stability and recyclability of the as-prepared nanostructures (i.e., CCNs-2, pristine CdS-NSs, and CQD/CdS-NSs) were investigated for three successive runs of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction. Considering a possible occurrence of Cr(OH)3 deposited on the surface of the used nanostructures, they were washed one time with nitrate acid solution (0.5 M) and three times with UPW [42], followed by the next recycle of photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) in saline water. All experiments in this study were performed in triplicates.
The DFT calculations were carried out to optimize the CQDs and three possible structures coordinated with CdS-NSs by applying the basis of Gaussian 09 Revision D. 01 package [53].

The XRD patterns of the as-prepared nanostructures were examined to evaluate their purity and crystallinity. As shown in Fig. 1 , the diffraction peaks of pristine CdS-NSs are at 25.1°, 26.7°, 28.4°, 36.9°, 44.1°, 48.1°, 52.0°, 70.4° and 75.8° that could be well indexed to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (201), (211) and (105) planes of the hexagonal phase of CdS (JCPDS No. 41-1049), respectively. This indicated that the pristine CdS was successfully synthesized. A broad XRD peak of CQDs appeared around 22.5°, implying the disordered structure of CQDs [54]. No characteristic peak attributed to CQDs was observed in the XRD patterns of CCNs and CQD/CdS-NSs (Fig. 1), which could be due to low CQDs content and high dispersion in the as-prepared nanostructures. As can be seen, the peaks of CCNs and CQD/CdS-NSs were consistent with those of the pristine CdS-NSs (Fig. 1), suggesting that the essential crystal structure of CdS maintained well after either implantation or deposition of CQDs.

The XPS spectra were performed to examine elemental compositions and their chemical states in the nanostructures. Elements Cd, S, and C were detected in the XPS survey spectra (Fig. 2 a). The peaks located at 404.5 eV and 411.3 eV for CdS-NSs were assigned to the binding energies of Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. 2b), which corresponded to the typical values for divalent cadmium (i.e., Cd2+) [55]. The binding energies at 403.4 eV and 410.2 eV were of Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 in low valance state or interstitial cadmium of CdS [56], consistent with the Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 of CdS in the CdS/CoOx heterostructures [39]. Similar characteristic peaks of Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 were observed in the CQDs/CdS-NSs and CCNs-2 (Fig. 2b), suggesting that the divalent cadmium of CdS remained even after implantation or deposition of CQDs in/on CdS-NSs. Interestingly, the peaks of Cd 3d for CCNs-2 shifted toward the lower binding energies (∼0.4 eV) compared to pristine CdS-NSs (Fig. 2b), which might be due to the CQDs implantation through -NH2 groups on the surface of CQDs [57].

FT-IR spectra were obtained to identify surface functional groups of CQDs. A slight absorbance peak located at the wavenumber of 3250 cm−1 was attributed to the -NH2 group (Fig. S1), indicating the occurrence of amine groups on the surface of CQDs. The peak of -NH2 group was still observed from CCNs-2, which was shifted to a lower wavenumber (∼3220 cm−1) (Fig. S1), implying that the CQDs were implanted into CdS-NSs through coordination of -NH2 group, which was further documented by DFT calculations. The geometry of CQDs is stimulated according to the structure shown in Fig. 3 a and the optimized Cd-N bond lengths are 1.374 Å, 1.493 Å, 1.375 Å, and 1.320 Å, respectively. Accordingly, vibrational analysis indicated that the NH stretch frequency difference observed with the redshift of 30 cm−1 agreed with the scaled calculated one with red-shifted 80 cm−1 from CQDs to CQDs-CdS (Fig. 3b). Unfortunately, another two complexing structures, based on the DFT calculations, did not agree with the experimental redshift of FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3c,d). As shown in Fig. S1, the peak of -NH2 group from CQD/CdS-NSs did not shift relative to that of CQDs, which was reasonable for the deposition of CQDs on CdS-NSs.

The S 2p for CdS-NSs could be deconvoluted into two peaks, locating at 160.2 eV (S 2p3/2) and 161.4 eV (S 2p1/2), respectively (Fig. 2c). The peak separation of 1.2 eV was consistent with the valence characteristic of metal sulfide [58], confirming the occurrence of CdS, which was well consistent with the XRD analysis. The CCNs-2 and CQD/CdS-NSs also exhibited a separation of 1.2 eV between S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 (Fig. 2c), suggesting that CdS remained regardless of CQDs implantation or deposition. Moreover, no shift was observed for the S 2p peaks of CCNs-2 relative to CdS-NSs and CQD/CdS-NSs (Fig. 2c). Together with the peak shift of Cd 3d, this suggested that the -NH2 groups on the CQDs were coordinated with Cd, therefore implanting CQDs in CdS-NSs and keeping the -NH2 groups. Thus, it was not surprising that the peaks of Cd 3d for CCNs-2 shifted toward the lower binding energies for ∼0.4 eV relative to pristine CdS-NSs. Also, the C 1s spectra of CCNs-2 and CQD/CdS-NSs revealed the presence of a peak at 284.6 eV, which was attributed to the CC/C=C band of the graphite structure of CQDs [59], consistent with the FT-IR analysis.

The prepared CdS composites were in the form of nanosheets, exhibiting ∼50 nm in width and ∼90 nm in length (Fig. 4 a). The CQDs were well dispersed on the ultra-thin film TEM grid with a size range of 3–5 nm (Figs. 4b and 4c). Both implantation and deposition of CQDs did not change the morphology of CdS-NSs (Fig. 4d and g). The HRTEM images of CCNs-2 showed that the quantum dot structures were distributed in the CdS-NSs, leading to micro-regional heterostructures between CQDs and CdS-NSs (Fig. 4e and f). The lattice fringe spacings of ∼0.22 and ∼0.33 nm were determined (Fig. 4e and f), corresponding to the {101} plane of CQDs and {002} plane of CdS, respectively [57,60]. CQDs with sizes of 3∼5 nm were observed from CQD/CdS-NSs (Fig. 4h), suggesting successful deposition of CQDs on the CdS-NSs. Taken together, these results demonstrated that both implantation and deposition of CQDs in/on the CdS-NSs were feasible.

The photocatalytic activity was performed by investigating the reduction of aqueous Cr(VI) under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 5 a, there are negligible changes in the concentration of aqueous Cr(VI) throughout the experimental period in the absence of the as-prepared nanostructures, indicating that aqueous Cr(VI) had no photolysis under visible light irradiation. Before performing photocatalytic experiments, the suspensions with photocatalysts were magnetically stirred for 30 min under dark conditions to establish an adsorption-desorption equilibrium; < 3% aqueous Cr(VI) was adsorbed. This implied that any dramatic decrease in the concentration of aqueous Cr(VI) would be primarily attributed to Cr(VI) reduction by photocatalysts under visible light irradiation.

The level of Cr(VI) in water containing pristine CdS-NSs decreased as a function of irradiation time, i.e., ∼25% and ∼14% of aqueous Cr(VI) remained at 10 and 20 min, respectively (Fig. 5a). Implantation of CQDs enhanced the photocatalytic activity of CdS-NSs; except CCNs-0.5, all the other CCNs nanostructures exhibited higher efficiencies of aqueous Cr(VI) removal compared to pristine CdS-NSs under the same conditions (Fig. 5a). In particular, the amount of CQD adding during the synthesis of CCNs was an important factor affecting the photocatalytic activity of CCNs. The CCNs-0.5 relative to pristine CdS-NSs showed a comparable efficiency of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction (Fig. 5a), which might be due to low CQDs content. With the increasing amount of CQD adding during CCNs synthesis, photocatalytic activity of CCNs increased first and then greatly decreased (Fig. 5a), suggesting that an appropriate amount of CQD dosage was crucial to achieve the optimal performance of fabricating CQDs-implanted CdS-NSs (i.e., CCNs). There was negligible change in the concentration of aqueous Cr(VI) in the presence of CQDs even under visible light irradiation (Fig. S2). Deposition of CQDs slightly enhanced the photocatalytic activity of CdS-NSs (Fig. 5b). In other words, depositing CQD/CdS-NSs nanostructures exhibited inferior efficiencies of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction (Fig. 5b).

As shown in Fig. 5a, CCNs-2 shows a superior photocatalytic activity among the CCNs nanostructures (i.e., negligible Cr(VI) could be detected in water after 10 min of light irradiation). It appeared that a small amount addition of CQD was not sufficient to implant appropriate CQDs in the CdS-NSs to promote the transfer of photogenerated charge carriers, thus resulting in a relatively low photocatalytic activity. In contrast, an amount larger than 2.0 mg of CQDs would reduce the photocatalytic efficiency of CdS-NSs because of an inferior photocatalytic activity of CQDs. Overall, these results demonstrated that implantation relative to deposition would be a superior mode for CQDs to improve the photocatalytic activity of CdS-NSs (Fig. 5b).

The linear kinetic curves of the first-order decay of Cr(VI) reduction by the as-prepared nanostructures are shown in Fig. 5c. The CCNs-2 had the highest reduction rate of Cr(VI) among the nanostructures (Fig. 5c). As shown in Fig. 5d, the rate constants of (0.91 ± 0.05) × 10−1 and (1.16 ± 0.03) × 10-1 min-1 were determined for pristine CdS-NSs and CQD/CdS-NSs, respectively. The CCNs-2 exhibited the highest rate constant of (3.75 ± 0.07) × 10-1 min-1, corresponding to ∼4 and ∼3 times higher that of pristine CdS-NSs and CQD/CdS-NSs, respectively. Comparatively, a recent study on photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) by ZnInS4 nanosheet-coated CdS nanorod heterostructures showed the highest rate constant of 1.79 × 10-1 min-1 [42], which is much lower than the value observed for CCNs-2 in our study.

The CCNs-2 as an optimal photocatalyst was further applied to water containing Cr(VI) and inorganic ions (or in salts of NaNO3, NaCl, and CaCl2) to examine the applicability of CCNs-2 in saline water. Results showed no change in the efficiency of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction when the content of inorganic ions increased to 500 mg/L (Figs. 6 a and 6b). Importantly, CCNs-2 still exhibited a high efficiency (∼94%) of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction within 10 min even in water with 1200 mg/L inorganic salts (Fig. 6c). The obtained rate constant was (2.62 ± 0.04) × 10−1 min-1 (Fig. S3). This demonstrated a good photocatalytic activity of the CCNs nanostructures towards Cr(VI) even in saline water.

Comparatively, pristine CdS-NSs and CQD/CdS-NSs showed inferior photocatalytic activity towards Cr(VI) in saline water after irradiation for 10 min. For example, ∼47% and ∼56% reduction of aqueous Cr(VI) in the presence of 1200 mg/L inorganic salts at rate constants of (0.58 ± 0.07) × 10−1 and (0.78 ± 0.09) × 10−1 min-1 for CdS-NSs and CQD/CdS-NSs, respectively (Figs. 6d and S3). Obviously, these results clearly demonstrated that CQDs-implanted CdS nanosheets (i.e., CCNs) had the high photocatalytic activity to reduce Cr(VI) even in saline water.

Organic matters in saline water could affect the photocatalytic activity of CCNs. The efficiencies of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction decreased with increasing concentrations of NOMs in water containing 1200 mg/L inorganic salts (Fig. S4a). This might be attributed to surface coverage by NOMs. It is reported that NOMs may tightly wrap nanosheets due to the high affinity and interactions [61]. For example, ∼9% decrease in the efficiency of Cr(VI) reduction was observed when the concentration of NOMs increased from 2.0 to 10 mg/L, yielding removal efficiencies of ∼82% and ∼73% for aqueous Cr(VI) after irradiation of 10 min (Fig. S4a), respectively.

The effect of pH on the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) is presented in Fig. S4b. Compared to near-neutral solutions (or pH 6.4), alkaline solutions at pH 8.0 and 10.1 reduced the efficiencies of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction by CCNs-2 in the presence of 1200 mg/L inorganic salts. At pH 4.1, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of reducing Cr(VI) in saline water was seen (Fig. S4b). This was consistent with the finding from a recent study under acidic conditions on the photocatalytic production of H2 from water by CdS nanostructures [62].

Finally, the stability and recyclability of the photocatalyst were investigated for its practical applications. The efficiencies of photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction by pristine CdS-NSs and deposited CQD/CdS-NSs decreased from 47.0% and 56.1% to 2.7% and 4.3% after 3 successive cycles (Fig. S5), respectively. The efficiency of CCNs-2 decreased from 94.9% to 78.1% under the same number of cycles (Fig. S5), suggesting that implantation relative to deposition of CQDs greatly improved the stability of CdS-NSs. To further examine its stability, XRD patterns of the used nanostructures were determined after 3 successive cycles. The characteristics of CdS diffraction peak were easily seen for CCNs-2 (Fig. S6), indicating that CdS in the CCNs-2 could still perform photocatalytic activity even after 3 times of recyclability. In contrast, XRD patterns of CdS in both pristine CdS-NSs and deposited CQD/CdS-NSs photocatalysts were nearly exhausted after 3 successive cycles (Fig. S6). Again, implantation of CQDs greatly enhanced the stability of CdS-NSs, suggesting that photocorrosion of CdS-NSs could be mitigated by implanting CQDs.

In the present study, it is hypothesized that implantation relative to deposition of CQDs could lead to superior photocatalytic activity and stability of CdS-NSs due to the modulation of band gap structure and improvement of charge separation. The hypothesis was first tested by the optical absorption properties of the as-prepared nanostructures. As shown in Fig. 7 a, pristine CdS-NSs exhibited a fundamental absorption edge at ∼530 nm, and a comparable absorption edge is observed for the deposited CQD/CdS-NSs. This suggested that deposition of CQDs was not able to change the optical absorption properties of CdS-NSs. The corresponding optical band gaps for pristine CdS-NSs and deposited CQD/CdS-NSs were estimated as ∼2.37 eV for each of them, based on UV–vis spectra (Fig. 7b). In contrast, an increase of ∼20 nm in the absorption edge was observed for implanted CCNs-2 (Fig. 7a), indicating that the absorption of visible light irradiation by Cd-NSs improved after implantation of CQDs, which may be ascribed to the formation of heterostructures between CQDs and CdS-NSs. It has been suggested that CQDs possessed electron-accepting and electron-transport properties [63,64], which makes CQDs-implanted hybrid photocatalysts as superior materials for harvesting visible light. Accordingly, CCNs-2 possessed a narrow band gap of ∼2.29 eV relative to pristine CdS-NSs and deposited CQD/CdS-NSs (Fig. 7b). This was well consistent with the phenomenon reported in previous studies on construction of heterostructures with CQDs [65,66]. This could further increase photo-generated electron-holes gap under visible light irradiation, which was beneficial to the enhancement of photocatalytic activity.

In the second set of experiments, EIS measurements were conducted to test the interfacial charge transfer performance of pristine CdS-NSs, CQD/CdS-NSs, and CCNs nanostructures. Results showed that the arc radius of CCNs-2 was much smaller than those of pristine CdS-NSs and CQD/CdS-NSs nanostructures (Figs. 8 a, 8b and S7). It is widely known that the smaller arc radius had higher efficiency in charge transfer [52,67]. Obviously, EIS data displayed a more efficient interfacial charge migration of the implanted CCNs-2 than pristine CdS-NSs and deposited CQD/CdS-NSs. This might be attributed to the excellent electrical conductivity of CQDs and the interface contact between backbone CdS-NSs and implanted CQDs. CQDs-implanted CdS nanosheets (i.e., CCNs-2) may provide well interfacial connection, which appeared to very crucial in promoting the charges transfer/separation and thus decreasing significantly photogenerated electrons and holes recombination. Taken together, it is proposed that the implantation of CQDs could effectively accelerate the separation and migration of the photoexcited electrons and holes, resulting in mitigation of electron-holes recombination, which thus improved the photocatalytic performance of CCNs-2 nanostructures.

Finally, the position of conduction band is an important parameter for the photocatalytic reduction capacity of a photocatalyst. The XPS valence band spectra of pristine CdS-NSs, deposited CQD/CdS-NSs, and implanted CCNs-2 were measured to characterize their valence band maximum (VBM). As shown in Fig. 9 a, the values of VBM are 0.30, 0.08 and 0.30 eV below the Fermi level for the as-prepared pristine CdS-NSs, CCNs-2, and CQD/CdS-NSs, respectively. The values of conduction band minimum (CBM) of these photocatalysts were also estimated based on their band gaps from the UV–vis spectra. The CCNs-2 exhibited the highest CBM position (Fig. 9b). This suggested that the CCNs-2 had the strongest reducible capability among the tested photocatalysts, thus resulting in an enhanced observed photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction under visible light irradiation.

Based on experimental and theoretical calculations, the enhanced photocatalytic mechanism of CCNs-2 was proposed (Scheme 1 ). Micro-regional heterostructures constructed through implantation of CQDs in CdS-NSs reduced the band gap, strengthening visible light absorption energetically. More importantly, the micro-regional heterostructures provided high photocarrier transfer kinetics to drive photogenerated electron-hole pairs spatial separation and thus decreased photogenerated electrons and holes recombination, thus improving the photocatalytic performance of CCNs-2 nanostructures.

We have successfully prepared CQDs-implanted CdS-NSs (i.e., CCNs) through solvothermal fabrication of freeze-dried CQDs and CdS-NS precursors. The synthesized CCNs-2 exhibited highly efficient photocatalytic activity of aqueous reduction of Cr(VI) in saline water under visible light irradiation, which could be possible through the decrease in band gap and efficient interfacial charge migration. The formation of micro-regional heterostructures in CQDs-implanted CdS-NSs caused the efficient migration. Compared to pristine CdS-NSs and CQDs-deposited CdS-NSs, CCNs-2 showed enhanced anti-photocorrosion and photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction. Furthermore, only minor decreases in the photocatalytic efficiency of CCNs-2 to reduce Cr(VI) in water was observed even in the presence of inorganic salts at a concentration of 1200 mg/L. This study provided evidence that implantation relative to deposition of CQDs would be a superior strategy to enhance the photocatalytic activity of CdS-NSs under visible light irradiation.
